segments.s676-g145: نظاره (nazāra) is a noun meaning 'watching' or 'gaze' and here is the head of the phrase. بران is the fused form of بر + آن ('on/upon that' or 'upon them') functioning as a postposition/pronominal complement attached after the noun: literally 'a watching upon them'. از (az) is the preposition 'from'; دو (two) modifies رویه (rūye) 'surface/side' (here 'flank' or 'side'); سپاه (sepāh) 'army' follows as the possessed noun in a genitive-like sequence (رویهٔ سپاه = 'the flanks of the army').
segments.s676-g146: که (ke) introduces the subordinate clause 'that/so/when'; تا (tā) here marks temporal extent 'until'. پهلوان (pahlevān) is the subject 'the champion/hero'. چون (chun) here functions as 'as/when' (classical usage meaning 'when' or 'as'); رود is the classical/poetic third-person singular present/subjunctive of رفتن (رَوَد) 'he goes/should go' (Ferdowsi uses this archaic form); نزد شاه (nazd-e shāh) means 'to/in the presence of the king' — نزد functions as a relational preposition 'to/near/in the presence of', and شاه is the object 'king'. The whole clause gives the time/extent up to which the watching occurred: 'until the champion went before the king.'
segments.s676-g25: چگونه is an interrogative adverb 'how'. نشیند is a present/subjunctive-like form of 'نشستن' (here 'to sit' — literary/poetic third-person singular), placed after the question word. بهنگام is a temporal preposition/adverb meaning 'at the time of', and بار here denotes the time/occasion of packing/loading or departure; together the phrase asks 'how could he sit at that time'.
segments.s676-g26: برفتن is the infinitive 'to depart/leave'; کند is the present-tense third-person singular 'does/makes' used here to express doing/undertaking (poetic style). هیچ is an emphatic negator 'no/none/at all', رای is 'plan/counsel' and شکار is 'hunt' — the clause therefore means 'and makes no plan to depart for the hunt'. Word order is verb-infinitive + auxiliary + negator + object (poetic compression).
segments.s676-g27: برفتند is a past plural verb 'they went/departed' placed before the following noun (verb‑first order common in poetry). کارآگهان (literally 'those informed') is a plural participial noun 'the informed men' or 'scouts/agents'. از + درش means 'from his gate/door' (در here = 'gate/door', ش = his).
segments.s676-g28: نبود is past 'there was not' (negated existence). آگه means 'aware/informed'. از کار = 'about the affair/the matter' (از here = 'concerning/of'), and وز لشکرش intends 'and of his army' (the text compresses conjunctions; the clause says there was no one aware of either the matter or his army). The overall sense is 'there was no one informed about the affair or about his host.'
segments.s676-g29: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. The three verbs رفتند، دیدند، بازآمدند are all past‑plural actions 'they went, saw, and returned' linked by و ('and'), forming a single temporal clause. The sequence describes a quick reconnaissance: they went, observed, and then came back.
segments.s676-g30: نهانی is an adverb 'secretly/covertly'. بر is the preposition 'upon/against/toward' governing او ('him'). فراز is an adverbial/adpositional element meaning 'from above / upon' (here contributing the sense 'came up on/over him'), and آمدند is past plural 'they came'. Word order places the adverb first, then the prepositional phrase and the verb, typical of poetic compression.
segments.s676-g31: که here introduces an explanatory/relative clause ('for'/'that'). لشکر is 'army/host'. بهرکار is an adjectival phrase 'ready for action' or 'fit for the task' describing the army. با او یکی‌ست is a contracted poetic expression meaning 'is one with him / was at his side' (اویکیست is the fused form of او یکی است). The whole clause: 'for the army, ready for action, was with him.'
segments.s676-g32: اگر ... وگر introduces a contrastive conditional: اگر = 'if', وگر = 'but if / or if'. نامدارست is 'is famed/renowned' (third-person copula form), and کودکیست is 'is a child' (the particle -ست marks copula). The clause offers two possibilities about the person's status: whether famous or a child — either way the army was one with him.
segments.s676-g137: Literal noun phrase: 'سه ترک دلاور' = 'three brave Turks' (number + noun + epithet, poetic placement of the adjective after the noun). 'ز خاقانیان' is a prepositional phrase ‘from/of the Khaghanians’ using the archaic/prepositional 'ز' (poetic equivalent of modern 'از').
segments.s676-g138: 'بران' is an adverb/pronoun meaning 'there/on that/against them' linking to the preceding group; the phrase 'کین بهرام بسته میان' uses 'کین' (vengeance/enmity) as the noun and 'بسته' as a past-participle/adjectival verbal 'bound/tied', followed by 'میان' ('between') to indicate location — literally, 'Bahram's vengeance bound between (them)'. Word order is poetic: noun + participle + locative.
segments.s676-g139: 'پذیرفته هر سه که' = 'all three have accepted/agreed that' (پذیرفته is past participle/predicate meaning 'have accepted'). The conjunction 'که' introduces the subordinate clause; 'چون' here functions as a temporal conjunction meaning 'when' (classical usage), and 'روی شاه' is a genitival construction 'the king's face' (روی = face). Note the poetic order: verb/predicate before the reflective subject 'هر سه'.
segments.s676-g140: 'ببینیم' is the present-subjunctive/hortatory form 'we see' or 'when we see' inside the subordinate clause introduced by 'چون'. 'دور از میان سپاه' is a prepositional phrase 'away from the midst of the army' — here 'دور' functions adverbially ('away/far'), and 'از میان' is the idiom 'from the midst (of)'.
segments.s676-g141: 'اگر' marks a conditional clause ('if'). 'بسته‌گر' is an agent-noun from 'بستن' meaning 'the binder/captor/jailer' (the man who has bound or holds someone). 'کشته' here is a past participle/verb 'kills' or 'has killed' used predicatively; 'او را' is the definite object 'him' with the object marker 'را'; 'برت' is an enclitic/old form meaning 'to/for you' (archaic second-person marker indicating the recipient). The clause thus lays out the condition: 'if the captor kills him'.
segments.s676-g142: 'بیاریم' is the verb 'we bring' (hortative/subjunctive/future sense: 'we will bring'). 'و' connects clauses ('and'). 'آسوده شد لشکرت' is a verbal clause where 'آسوده شد' = 'became relieved/at ease' and 'لشکرت' = 'your army' (possessive suffix -ت). Note the predicate precedes the noun subject in poetic word order: 'was relieved, your host'.
segments.s676-g143: 'ز یک روی' = 'from one side' / 'on one side' (poetic prepositional phrase: ز + numeral + noun). 'خسرو' names the king; 'دگر پهلوان' = 'the other, the champion' (دگر = the other/another). This is a balanced, parallel construction contrasting the two sides.
segments.s676-g144: 'میان اندرون نهروان' = 'into the midst / inner part of Nahrawan' (میان = between/in the midst, اندرون = inside/interior, نهروان = place-name). 'روان' here is a predicative/adjectival/participial form meaning 'moving/proceeding' or 'they went' (poetic; the verbal sense 'proceeding' is implied with the plural subject), so the whole segment says that between those two figures they proceeded into Nahrawan's inner quarters.
segments.s676-g81: اگر introduces a conditional clause ('if'). بشنود is the classical/polite present-subjunctive (or potential) form of شنیدن placed before the subject (verb‑first order typical in poetic/classical Persian) and here means 'he hears' / 'should hear'. شهریار is the subject 'the king' and این سخن is the object 'this speech' (object after subject in this line for rhythm).
segments.s676-g82: که is the relative/complementizer 'that' linking to 'این سخن'. گفته‌ست is the perfect/periphrastic form (گفته + ست, with ست = archaic/poetic form of است) meaning 'has said' or 'has spoken'. بیدار modifies مرد ('awake/alert man') and کهن is a further adjective 'old/ancient'; the adjective sequence is inverted for poetic effect but reads as 'the old, alert man' who uttered the speech.
segments.s676-g83: بدو is the dative/adverbial 'to him' (to that person). گفت here is the past-tense verb 'said' (poetic word order places it before the subject). شاه is the subject 'the king'. این سخن‌گر بزر is the object phrase 'this eloquent / venerable speaker' (بزر used as 'venerable/elder' after the noun for poetic emphasis).
segments.s676-g84: نویسم is first-person present/subjunctive 'I write' or 'I will write' (a declarative statement by the king). جز این نیست is a negative existential pattern literally 'there is nothing except this' (جز + این + نیست). آیین و فر is the nominal predicate 'law/custom (آیین) and royal glory (فر)', so the clause means 'there is nothing as law/custom and glory except this.' Word order is compacted for poetic meter.
segments.s676-g85: سخن گفتن is a verbal-noun construction 'the speaking / speech' functioning as the subject. موبدان follows to mark the possessor ('the priests' — موبدان = mōbeds). گوهرست = گوهر + ست (ست = poetic form of است) is the predicate 'is a jewel' (a metaphor praising the priests' speech).
segments.s676-g86: مرا در دل اندیشه دیگرست is an existential/experiential statement literally 'for me in the heart a different thought is' — idiomatically 'I have another thought in my heart.' مـرا (here the cliticized form 'مرا') marks the experiencer/possessor, در دل 'in (the) heart', اندیشه 'thought', and دیگرست = دیگر + ست = 'is different/other'.
segments.s676-g87: که چون together introduce the subordinate temporal/conditional clause ('when' / 'if'); چون here functions as 'when/once'. این دو لشکر = 'these two armies'. برابر شود uses برابر (equal/matched) with شود (subjunctive/passive auxiliary) meaning 'become equal' or 'stand equal' — a typical poetic clause for 'when the two armies are evenly matched.'
segments.s676-g88: سر نیزه‌ها is the (plural) subject 'the heads/tips of the spears' (نیزه‌ها with the plural suffix). بر is the preposition 'on/upon/against', دو پیکر 'two bodies' (پیکر = body, here likely 'corpses' in battle imagery), and شود again is the potential/subjunctive 'will be' or 'become.' The whole clause means 'the spear‑heads will be upon two bodies.'
segments.s676-g41: 'چو' = 'when/whenever' introducing a temporal clause. 'از رزم' = 'from the battle' (preposition + noun), and 'شاهان' is the object/target (poetic inversion places the modifier before the noun). 'نراند' is the past/hegemonic narrative form 'he drove (away)'; with the enclitic/particle 'همی' after the verb it gets a habitual/continued sense ('would drive/kept driving') common in classical Persian.
segments.s676-g42: 'همه' here = 'the whole/entire' and modifies 'دفتر' (book). 'دفترِ دمنه' is a genitival phrase 'the book of Damanah' (proper-name use of دمنه in this context); 'خواند' is the past verb 'read/recited' and 'همی' again marks habitual or repeated action ('would read'). Word order (modifier → noun → verb) is typical of Persian poetry.
segments.s676-g43: 'چنین' = 'thus/so' modifying the speech verb. 'گفت' is the speech verb 'said' (here listed as a content word but used as the simple past verb). 'خسرو' is the speaker (proper name). 'بدستور' = 'by order' (prepositional phrase), and 'خویش' is 'his own' (formed here by 'خوی' + the possessive clitic 'ش'); the whole phrase means 'by his own command'.
segments.s676-g44: 'که' introduces the content clause ('that/that …'). 'کاری' = 'a task/work' (here lexical sense not listed in the provided senses). 'درازست' = 'is long' (duração/temporal length). 'ما را' marks 'us' as the affected party (object/experiencer) with 'را' as the object/definite marker/emphasis. 'به پیش' = 'ahead / before us' — together: 'that a long task lies ahead of us.'
segments.s676-g45: 'چو' = 'when/as'. 'بهرام' is the subject (proper name). 'بر دشمن' = 'against the enemy' ('بر' used with motion/attack = 'upon/against'). 'اسپ' = 'steed/horse' is the direct object of 'افکند' = 'he threw/hurled' (simple past); the clause describes a sudden martial action.
segments.s676-g46: 'بدریا' = 'into/at the sea' (a locative/poetic prepositional form). 'دل اژدها' = 'the dragon's heart' (possessive/genitive order). 'بشکند' is the third-person verb form with the present/optative prefix 'ب-' (in classical/narrative contexts this form often reports a sudden/resultant action and functions like a past narrative: 'was broken' or 'broke'). Together: 'then the dragon's heart broke (at/in) the sea.'
segments.s676-g47: 'دگر' = 'another/other'. 'آنک' is a relative pronoun 'who/that' (poetic form). 'آیین' = 'customs/rites' and 'شاهنشهان' = 'of the shahanshahs/emperors' (construct/genitive understood): 'the customs/rites of the emperors.'
segments.s676-g48: 'بیاموخت' is the classical past form 'he learned' (bi- prefix + verb root) — active 'learned/was taught'. 'از' = 'from', 'شهریار' = 'the king/sovereign' and 'جهان' = 'the world' (epithet 'sovereign of the world'). Together: 'he learned … from the world's sovereign.'
segments.s676-g73: A nominal phrase without a verb: دگر 'another/other' (preposed modifier) + بهره 'share/portion' + مردم 'people' + پارسا 'pious' (adjective after the noun). Word order is archaic but straightforward: 'another share (is) for pious people' with the copula omitted as is normal in Persian poetry.
segments.s676-g74: سدیگر here serves as 'and another / the other' (poetic connector introducing the next share). پرستنده is a participle/noun 'worshipper, devotee' and پادشا 'king' follows it; together they mean 'another [share] (to) the king who is worshipped' (a nominal clause with implied 'belongs to').
segments.s676-g75: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. نزدیک باشد is a subordinate present clause using باشد (subjunctive/present auxiliary) meaning 'is near'. بشاه جهان is an older/poetic form meaning 'the king of the world' (بشاه = شاه with an archaic prefix/particle and جهان postpositively specifying 'of the world').
segments.s676-g76: خرد خویشتن = 'one's own wisdom' (خویشتن is an emphatic reflexive marking possession). ز + و is the poetic contraction of از او 'from him'. ندارد نهان is literal 'does not have hidden' and functions idiomatically as 'is not hidden' (ندارد = 'does not have/there is not', نهان = 'hidden'). Together: 'his own wisdom is not hidden from him.'
segments.s676-g77: کنون 'now' sets the time. از خرد پاره‌ای (archaic word order) = 'a part/piece of wisdom' (more usual modern order: پاره‌ای از خرد). ماند is the present/past verb 'remains / is left'. The final خرد repeats the noun for emphasis/apposition: 'a piece of wisdom remains — (only) wisdom (i.e. little wisdom remains)'.
segments.s676-g78: که is the relative pronoun 'which/that'. دانا 'the wise (one)' is the subject of شمرد (reckoned/considered). ورا is the object pronoun 'it' (i.e. that remaining piece), بهر 'for/to' indicates purpose/recipient, دهقان 'peasant/farmer' is the beneficiary, and شمرد is past-tense 'counted/considered'. The whole: 'which the wise considered (to be) for the peasant.'
segments.s676-g79: A negated existential/predicative clause: خرد نیست 'wisdom is not' + با مردم ناسپاس 'with/united to ungrateful people' — meaning 'wisdom does not exist with ungrateful folk.' The negative نیست negates the existence/possession of خرد.
segments.s676-g80: نه introduces a second negative clause 'nor'. آنرا 'that (thing/it)' is resumed by the relative clause که او نیست یزدان‌شناس = 'who/for whom he is not God-knowing'. Here the sense is 'nor is it (wisdom) for one who is not pious/God-knowing' (یزدان‌شناس = a person who knows/fears God).
segments.s676-g33: هرانگه که is a subordinating temporal phrase meaning 'whenever/when.' لشکر is the subject 'the army/host.' راند (براند) is a poetic/present form meaning 'drives/marches (forward).' به راه is a prepositional phrase 'on/to the road' that follows the verb; Persian poetry allows this flexible order.
segments.s676-g34: بود here is past ('there was') introducing an existence clause. یک زمان is an adverbial phrase 'for a time' or 'a while.' در میان سپاه is a locative phrase 'in the midst of the host' (در میان = 'among/in the middle of').
segments.s676-g35: زمانی شود is an episodic/time expression 'at one time / it becomes' (شدن = 'become/turn'). بر سوی میمنه is a directional phrase 'toward the right side/flank' with بر/سوی marking direction and میمنه 'right side' as the target.
segments.s676-g36: گهی ... و گاه ... is a paired adverbial contrast meaning 'now and then / sometimes ... and sometimes ...'. بر چپ uses بر + noun to indicate direction 'to/on the left.' سوی بنه is another directional phrase 'toward beneh'; بنه here is uncertain/poetic (likely a place or flank), so the best reading is directional but the exact noun sense is unclear.
segments.s676-g37: همه مردم is 'all the people / every people.' خویش + ـش is a possessive construction meaning 'their own/custom/character' and دارد is the present tense 'has/possesses.' براز is a concrete noun 'dung/manure' used here literally or proverbially.
segments.s676-g38: ببیگانگانشان is the compound pronoun+noun 'their strangers/foreigners.' نیاید is negative-present 'does not come / does not reach' (here idiomatically 'they gain no'), and نیاز is 'need / benefit' — the clause says that foreigners do not obtain/receive that need/benefit.
segments.s676-g39: بکردار شاهان is an adjectival phrase 'with the bearing/conduct of kings' (ب- prefix + کردآر = 'in manner/behaviour'). نشیند is a present/poetic form 'sits' (third-person), and ببار is a jussive/imperative-form verb 'let it rain / it pours' used here in a compressed poetic clause following the subject.
segments.s676-g40: همان marks 'that same' or 'he/it thus.' در در is a poetic pairing where در is used as a landscape noun (here likely 'forest/wood') repeated for rhythm; دشت is 'plain.' جوید is present 'seeks/searches for,' and شکار is 'prey/hunt' — together 'seeks game in forest and plain.' Note: the noun sense of در (forest) is poetic and not listed in the provided senses.
segments.s676-g65: بگویید is an imperative plural/polite form 'tell (me/us)'. تا introduces a subordinate clause (here functioning like English 'what/that' to introduce an indirect question). چاره کار چیست is an inverted nominal question: چاره (remedy/solution) + کار (of the matter) with the copula چیست ('what is') placed at the end (classical word order for indirect questions).
segments.s676-g66: This clause asks who causes the suffering. بران is a prepositional/relational element 'on/to those' (linking to خستگیها). خستگیها is the plural 'wearinesses/fatigues' and پرآزار is an adjective 'very troublesome' placed before the copula. کی + ست is the interrogative 'who is' (کیست), formed by کی 'who' plus the copula ست/است ('is'), with classical inversion of adjective before the verb.
segments.s676-g67: بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him' (to + third person). گفت is the past tense 'said' and here takes موبد 'the mobad/priest' as the following subject (verb–subject order is common in classical Persian). انوشه is a proper name (vocative/subject). بدی in this classical context is an archaic past tense form (second-person past of دادن, 'you gave') rather than the nominal 'evil' — it appears as the verb of the reported speech; this is an older morphological form common in epic Persian.
segments.s676-g68: همه مغز را is the definite object phrase 'to/for all the magi' (مغز here is the classical plural of مغ 'magi'; را marks the definite object). فر وتوشه is a compound noun phrase 'farr (royal glory) and tosheh (provisions/treasure)'. بدی is again the archaic past-tense verb 'gave' whose object is همه مغز را; word order is Verb final within the reported clause (typical of Ferdowsi).
segments.s676-g69: چوپیدا شد is a temporal clause 'when (it) was found/become found' (چو = 'when', پیدا شد = 'was found'). این راز is the demonstrative + noun 'this secret'. گردنده دهر is an agent phrase 'the turner/rotator of the age' (a poetic epithet) and functions as the subject performing the following verb; in Ferdowsi the subject often follows the temporal clause.
segments.s676-g70: خرد را is the definite object 'wisdom' (را marks definiteness/emphasis). ببخشید is the (classical) past tense of بخشیدن here in the sense 'bestowed/gave'. بر چاربهر is a prepositional phrase 'upon the four quarters' (i.e. everywhere/the four corners of the world). The verb agrees with the preceding subject (گردنده دهر) to express that the world-turner granted wisdom everywhere.
segments.s676-g71: چونیمی is a fused adverbial phrase 'چون نیمی' meaning 'as/for half (of it)' or 'since half'. ازو is the old genitive/pronominal 'of it/from it'. بهره is 'share' or 'portion'. پادشاست is the contracted predicate 'is the king's/pertains to the king' (پادشا + است in classical form), saying that that portion belongs to/concerns the king.
segments.s676-g72: که introduces an explanatory clause ('for/because' or 'that'). فر و خرد is a coordinated phrase 'farr (royal glory) and wisdom'. پادشا را is 'to the king' with را marking the definite/theme. سزاست is the predicate 'is fitting/deserved' (it states that farr and wisdom are what the king merits). The clause gives the reason why half belongs to the king.
segments.s676-g1: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning “when.” بشنید is past 3rd-singular “he heard.” The phrase is headed by the proper name بهرام as the subject; کز روزگار is a relative/prepositional phrase “from/of fate” that points forward to what will be reported in the next segment.
segments.s676-g2: چه آمد is a past interrogative/exclamatory turn of phrase meaning “what came / what happened.” بران (= بر + آن) means “upon that” and links back to the referent ‘that (king).’ نامور شهریار is the noun phrase “that renowned king.” The verb آمد is simple past and governs the clause.
segments.s676-g3: نهادند is past 3rd-plural “they placed/put.” بر introduces the locus “on/upon,” here بر چشم روشنش “upon his bright eye”; روشنش is روشن + possessive -ش “his bright,” and داغ is the object meaning a mark/brand or scar placed on the eye. Word order places the verb before the prepositional phrase and object, typical in classical Persian.
segments.s676-g4: بمرد is an optative/past-form meaning roughly “(there) perished/was extinguished” or a lamenting ‘may it die’ used poetically. آن چراغ دو نرگس is a kenning: “that lamp of two narcissi” (i.e. a pair of eyes); بباغ is locative “in the garden.” The clause reports the extinguishing of that poetic ‘lamp.’
segments.s676-g5: پسر is the subject “the son.” برنشست is past 3rd-singular “sat upon/took a seat.” از بر تخت اوی functions as a locative phrase “upon his throne,” where از بر is a classical prepositional combination and اوی is the possessive “his” attached to تخت (“throne”).
segments.s676-g6: بپا اندر آمد is a verbal phrase “rose up / came to stand.” سر وبخت اوی is a coordinated noun phrase “his head and (his) fortune” meaning his dignity/status and his luck. آمد is past, so the whole clause states that his status and fortune rose (i.e. improved) when he took the throne.
segments.s676-g7: ازان (from that) sets the starting point; ماند is past 3rd-singular “remained/was left.” بهرام is the subject and اندر شگفت is a prepositional phrase “in wonder/amazement,” so the whole means he remained in astonishment from that moment.
segments.s676-g8: بپژمرد is past 3rd-singular “he withered/wasted away.” واندیشه is و + اندیشه “and (by) thought”; اندر گرفت is a light verb construction literally “took hold within,” with گرفت as past “seized/took.” Together they mean “and thought took hold of him” (he was overtaken by thought/worry).
segments.s676-g129: نشست is a past 3rd‑person singular verb ('he sat/mounted'). از here is the preposition 'from/out of' used in a poetic locative/partitive sense introducing the thing he sat on: 'from/onto the [horse]'. برابلق مشک is a compound noun phrase ('the piebald, musk‑scented [horse]') with مشک qualifying برابلق; دم (literally 'breath' here) emphasizes the musk scent. Together this segment = 'he sat on the piebald, musk‑scented one.'
segments.s676-g130: This is a string of adjectives plus a noun that describe the hoof: خنیده is a perfect/participial sense ('sounded/resounded' or 'having sounded') used poetically, سرافراز is an adjective 'lofty/proudly‑raised', رویینه is an adjective 'armored/iron‑clad', and سم is the noun 'hoof'. Grammatically these postpositive modifiers attach to سم and complete the image begun in the previous segment.
segments.s676-g131: سلیحش is the possessed noun 'his weapon' (سلیح + possessive suffix -ش). یکی ... بود is a common copular construction meaning 'one (of them) was ...' — here یکی simply 'one' (i.e. one of his weapons). هندوی تیغ is the predicate nominative 'an Indian sword'; بود is the past copula 'was'.
segments.s676-g132: که introduces a relative clause modifying the sword ('which/that'). درزخم is a locative/prepositional phrase 'in the wound' (i.e. when it cut), چون = 'like', آتش = 'fire', میغ = 'blade' (here the blade is compared to fire), and بود is the copula 'was' ending the clause. Word order is typical: prepositional phrase + simile + noun + copula.
segments.s676-g133: چوبرق is a simile/adverbial phrase 'like lightning' (چو + برق fused poetically), درفشان is adjectival 'shining/glittering' (applied to the rider or his movement), همی‌راند is the durative past 'was driving' (همی‑ prefix marks ongoingness in epic narration), and اسپ is the direct object 'steed/horse'. The phrase describes the manner and action of riding.
segments.s676-g134: بدست here means 'in the hand' (i.e. held in his hand); چپش is 'his left (side/hand)' (possessive -ش). The following words are proper names in apposition (ریمن آذرگشسپ), so the whole segment lists what he held in his left hand: the named item/person. The copula/verb is supplied by context (i.e. '(was) in his left hand').
segments.s676-g135: چو functions temporally here as 'when'. آیین گشسپ is a genitive phrase 'Goshasp's custom/practice' (آیین = custom/practice). و joins یلان سینه ('the heroes of the breast', i.e. brave‑hearted heroes); نیز = 'also'. Word order places the temporal marker چو at the start and the coordinated noun phrases before the main verb that follows.
segments.s676-g136: برفتند is the 3rd‑person plural past 'they went/they marched forward'. The adjectives/phrases پرکینه و پرستیز follow the verb to describe their state — 'full of hatred' (پرکینه) and 'full of pomp/display' (پرستیز). This postverbal placement of predicative adjectives is idiomatic in epic narration.
segments.s676-g89: نباشد is an optative/negative form meaning roughly “may it not be / it would not be”; here it introduces the speaker’s claim that something would not count as shame. م and را together form the clitic object 'مرا' (“to me”), but the data list splits them — this segment therefore reads ‘it would not be shame to me’. کز is the poetic preposition ‘from/that from’ linking قلبگاه (‘the heart / core’) as the source or seat of whatever follows.
segments.s676-g90: برانم is a first‑person verbal form (subjunctive/optative flavor) from راندن ‘to drive/expel’ and here functions as part of the event described (to be driven away/from). شوم is the first‑person singular present/subjunctive of شدن ‘to become’ and here follows برانم to indicate the speaker’s resulting state. پیش او = “in the presence of him / before him.” بی‌سپاه is a compound adjective/adverbial phrase meaning “without an army / unescorted”; it modifies the resulting state.
segments.s676-g91: بخوانم is first‑person singular (subjunctive/optative/intentional) of خواندن used here as ‘I will call/summon’. به آواز literally ‘in/with the voice’ functions adverbially: “with the cry/voice of …”. بهرام را is the object of بخوانم — the name shouted or invoked; را is the definite/object marker highlighting Bahram as the person summoned.
segments.s676-g92: سپهدار (‘sepahdār’) is a title meaning ‘commander’ and is the head noun being described; بدنام is an adjective ‘notorious/infamous’ modifying سپهدار; خودکام is another adjective ‘autocratic, self‑willed’ further qualifying him. The final را is the object marker attaching to the whole nominal phrase, marking it as the object of the preceding verb (بخوانم).
segments.s676-g93: یکی here means ‘one / once / a single (time)’ — an adverbializing numeral meaning ‘once’ or ‘one act’. ز آشتی = ‘from/as to reconciliation’ — a short genitive/causal phrase meaning ‘as a sign of truce’ or ‘in a reconciliatory mood’. روی بنمایمش is a verbal phrase: روی ‘face’ is the object/thing shown, and بنمایمش (ب + نمای + م + ش) is a compound verb meaning ‘I will display/show it to him’ (the clitic ­-مش indexes the person shown).
segments.s676-g94: نوازمش is a first‑person verbal form meaning ‘I will caress/treat kindly/flatter him’ (نوازش + clitic). بسیار here modifies نوازمش and means ‘very much / greatly’. و is the coordinating conjunction ‘and’. بستایمش is again first person ‘I will praise him’ (بستودن/ستودن + clitic). The sequence gives two parallel actions of kindness and praise.
segments.s676-g95: اگر introduces a conditional ‘if’. خود ‘himself’ emphasizes the subject (he personally). پذیرد is subjunctive/present of پذیرفتن ‘to accept’; here it means ‘accepts’. سخن ‘speech / proposal / the talk’ is the thing offered. به بود is an idiomatic classical collocation where بود (past/optative of بودن) gives a judgemental comment — ‘it would be well / that would do’ — so the whole clause reads ‘if he himself accepts the proposal, that would be good/fitting.’
segments.s676-g96: که begins this explanatory/relative clause ‘for/that’. چون ‘like/as’ introduces the comparison ‘such as’. او is ‘he’. بدرگاه ‘to the royal court / presence’ names the place; بر here has the sense ‘to/toward’ and combines with بدرگاه. The final که بود is an archaic way to form a rhetorical question or relative sense: literally ‘who was (there)?’ — so the clause asks rhetorically, ‘for who was at court like him?’
segments.s676-g113: چو ... شد is a classical temporal clause meaning "when"; here چو = "when" and شد is the past of شدن "to become." The noun phrase شمع جهان (literally "candle of the world") is the subject and بخم is an archaic/poetic adjectival/participial element meaning "dim/obscure," placed after the verb; اندرون = "within/inside" specifies location.
segments.s676-g114: بیفشاند is the poetic present/preterital form of فشاندن with the prefixed ب- (here translated as "casts/sheds/scatters"). زلف شب is a metaphorical construct "the locks of night," where زلف (lock of hair) is used figuratively and شب = "night." تیره‌گون is a compound adjective (تیره + گون) meaning "dark-hued / dark-like," postposed to describe زلف.
segments.s676-g115: بیامد is the simple past "came." طلایه means "vanguard/forefront." ز هر دو سپاه is a prepositional phrase "from (each of) the two hosts" (here: "from both armies"); هر دو is the idiom "both/each of the two." Word order: subject (طلایه) + verb + source phrase.
segments.s676-g116: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause modifying طلایه ("which/for which"). دارد is present tense "has/keeps" indicating a habitual state. ز here means "against/from (because of)" and بدخواه خود را نگاه is the object phrase: بدخواه ("ill-wisher/enemy") qualified by خود ("his own") with را as the definite/object marker; نگاه ("gaze/watch") is the noun being taken—i.e. "keeps a watch against his enemy."
segments.s676-g117: چو = "when." از introduces the agent/source "from," so از خنجر روز = "from the dagger of day" (a metaphor for daylight's sharpness). بگریخت is the past perfective/preterite "fled" (3rd singular); شب is the subject "night." The clause means "when night fled from the day's dagger."
segments.s676-g118: همی is a classical adverb indicating continued or habitual action ("still/continually"). تاخت is the past of تاختن "to rush/gallop." The following phrase lists two results/effects with participial/adjectival words: سوزان دل = "burning the heart" (سوزان = "burning/heart‑rending" modifying دل) and خشک لب = "parching/drying the lip" (خشک modifying لب). No را is used; these nouns are direct objects/effected parts.
segments.s676-g119: برآمد is the past "arose/issued forth." تبیره is a rare/poetic noun here meaning a dark portent/obscurity (an archaic usage) and ز هر دو سرای means "from both quarters/houses." The whole clause says that a portentous/dark thing rose from both places. (Note: سرای is used poetically for "quarters/houses/places." )
segments.s676-g120: بدان is the classical fused form of به + آن meaning "to/for that." رزم = "battle"; خورشید is the subject "the sun." بد رهنمای is a noun phrase "a bad (بد) guide (رهنمای)": word order places the qualifier بد before the noun. The segment reads "to that battle the sun was a bad guide," i.e. the sun proved an unfortunate/misleading guide in that fight.
segments.s676-g17: 'فرستاد' is the third‑person past verb 'he sent/dispatched' placed before its object (a common poetic word order). 'بیدار' is an attributive adjective 'alert/awake' modifying 'کارآگهان' (a plural noun 'those aware' or 'investigators'); the whole phrase is the direct object of 'فرستاد'.
segments.s676-g18: The sequence 'که تا' introduces a purpose clause ('so that'); 'بازجویند' is a third‑person plural present/subjunctive form meaning 'they seek/investigate' used after the purposive marker. 'کارجهان' is the object of their seeking and here functions as the compound 'کارِ جهان' — 'the affairs/work of the world'.
segments.s676-g19: 'به' marks the indirect object ('to the informed men'); 'کارآگهان' here is the addressees 'the informed/knowing ones'; 'گفت' is the past tense verb 'he said/told' followed by its direct object 'راز' ('secret'); 'ازنخست' is an adverbial phrase 'from the first/from the outset' qualifying the temporal scope of the secret.
segments.s676-g20: The fused form 'زلشکر' should be read as 'زِ لشکر' ('from/of the army') and names what must be acted on. 'همی‌کرد' is the classical imperfect/habitual form ('would do/should do' or 'was to do') with the prefix همی‑; 'باید' is the modal 'must/it is necessary', and 'درست' is the adverb/adjective 'properly/right'. Together they express necessity: 'one must set right/make proper (the army)'.
segments.s676-g21: 'که' introduces the reported clause 'that...'. 'بااو' is the prepositional phrase 'with him' (poetically contracted). 'یکی اند' is a predicate 'are one/united' with 'اند' the plural copula coming after the predicate; 'لشکر به جنگ' specifies the subject and purpose ('the army for battle'), the noun 'لشکر' follows for emphasis and agrees with 'اند'.
segments.s676-g22: 'وگر' (= 'and if') introduces a conditional; 'گردد' is a third‑person singular subjunctive/optative of 'شدن' meaning 'becomes/happens'. 'این کار ما' is the noun phrase 'this affair of ours' (کار governed by the pronominal modifier ما), and 'با درنگ' is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'with delay'. The clause thus states a conditional result about their affairs becoming delayed.
segments.s676-g23: 'دگر' is an adverb introducing an additional point ('another/moreover'). 'آنک' is a relative/conjunctive particle 'that/where/which' introducing the clause about Bahram. 'بهرام' is the subject and 'در قلبگاه' is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the heart/core/central stronghold' (در + قلبگاه).
segments.s676-g24: 'بود' is the past copula 'was'. 'بیشتر‌گر' functions here as an adverbial intensifier 'more' or 'more so' (an older/poetic form), and 'میان سپاه' means 'among the army/host' — together describing that he was relatively more present/active among the troops.
segments.s676-g9: بفرمود (third-person past) is a polite/honorific past verb meaning 'he commanded/said'; تا here introduces purpose/result ('so that' / 'that'), and برند is a subjunctive/imperative-like third-person plural verb ('they bring/carry') depending on the sequence of past narrative—together بفرمود تا + 3pl is 'he ordered that they should ...'. کوس بیرون = 'the drum out' places the object (کوس) before the verb and uses بیرون as a directional/adverbial element 'out/away'.
segments.s676-g10: درفش بزرگی is a noun phrase 'a great banner/standard' with the adjective/noun بزرگی after درفش (poetic word order). به is a directional/prepositional marker 'towards/into', هامون is the goal/place 'the plain', and برند is the same 3pl verb 'they carry/bring' completing the purpose clause introduced by تا.
segments.s676-g11: This clause has a sequence of two simple past actions. بنه برنهاد is a nominal phrase (بنه) + past verb برنهاد 'he placed/setting up' (perfective past). وسپه برنشست is a coordinated clause where وسپه (the army/host) is the subject and برنشست is a past intransitive verb 'took a seat / sat down' — the conjunction/and is implied by the coordination.
segments.s676-g12: بپیکار here is a prefixed verbal/adverbial form meaning 'for battle/into fighting' (marks purpose or circumstance). خسرو is the subject/name, میان را is a direct object with را marking definiteness/emphasis ('the middle/centre'), and ببست is the past-tense verb 'he closed/shut' — together 'Khosrow closed the middle (of his line) for the fighting.'
segments.s676-g13: This nominative/noun phrase strings modifiers before/after the head: سپاهی (host/army) is the head noun, بکردار is a descriptive adjective 'virtuous/valiant' placed after it in this poetic order, and کوه روان is a compound/phrase 'mountain-moving' (کوه + روان) that qualifies the force as huge/moving like a mountain; روان here is an adjectival/participial form 'flowing/moving'.
segments.s676-g14: همی‌راند is an imperfect/progressive-form verb 'was driving/was advancing' (همی- marks continuous action in classical Persian). گستاخ is a postpositive adjective 'bold/audacious' describing how they advanced. تا is an extent marker 'as far as', and نهروان is the place name 'Nahrawan' serving as the terminal of motion.
segments.s676-g15: چوآگاه شد is a temporal clause: چوآگاه 'when (he was) informed/aware' + شد (past auxiliary/verb 'became') yields 'when he became aware/informed'. خسرو is the subject. از takes the following noun as the source/concern 'of/regarding', and کاراوی is the noun 'Karavi/that efficient one' or 'the action of Karavi' as the thing he was informed about.
segments.s676-g16: غمی گشت is a simple past-result clause: غمی (grief) as subject/theme + گشت (became/turned into) → 'grief came upon him'. زان is a connective 'from that/therefore', introducing the consequence clause; تیز بازار اوی is a compact noun phrase where تیز ('sharp/keen') modifies بازار ('market/affairs') and اوی is a possessive 'his' — together 'his keen/brisk affairs' (classical Persian often uses بازار metaphorically for busy activity).
segments.s676-g105: تو را باد is an optative construction: تو (you) + را (object marker/emphatic) + باد (optative particle = 'may it be'). Here باد introduces a wish. پیروزی و فرهی are the first two items of that wish; فرهی is an abstract epithet ('nobility, noble renown') coordinated with پیروزی by و.
segments.s676-g106: This continues the list of wished things. بزرگی ('greatness') is another noun in the series; دیهیم qualifies شاهنشهی and forms a compound sense with it (an epithet + the abstract noun 'kingship/empire'). The sequence is noun (بزرگی) + و + modifier + noun (دیهیم شاهنشهی) completing the optative objects.
segments.s676-g107: چنین گفت خسرو is a reported-speech frame: چنین ('thus') + گفت (past 'said') + خسرو (subject following the verb, common in poetry). که introduces the content clause. این باد و بس is a terse optative: این ('this') + باد (optative 'may it be') + و بس ('and enough' = 'may this suffice').
segments.s676-g108: شکست و جدایی are the negative outcomes mentioned ('defeat and separation'). مبیناد is an archaic/polite present-subjunctive/optative form of 'seeing/appearing' (here 'may ... appear/ be seen'); in this idiom it yields 'may no one see/experience'. کس after the verb functions as the affected person and here means 'no one'. The clause thus expresses a wish that no one should witness defeat or separation.
segments.s676-g109: سپه را is the definite direct object (سپه 'army' + را object marker). ز بغداد ('from Baghdad') is a prepositional source phrase (ز = 'from'). بیرون کشید is the past tense verb phrase 'drew/pulled out' (کشید here = 'drew/led out'). Word order is object — source — verb, which is frequent in epic poetry.
segments.s676-g110: سراپرده نو is a noun + adjective ('a new inner curtain/inner chamber'). به هامون is a directional phrase ('to/onto the plain'); here به means 'toward/into' and هامون names the plain. کشید repeats the verb 'drew/extended' (here 'set up' or 'pitched'), referring back to the act of placing the tent.
segments.s676-g111: دو لشکر is the subject phrase ('two armies'). چو here means 'when' introducing a temporal clause. تنگ اندر آمد is an idiomatic sequence meaning 'came close together' (تنگ = 'close/narrow', اندر آمد = 'came in/arrived'), and به راه ('on the road') marks the location/manner.
segments.s676-g112: ازان رو is a discourse connector 'thereupon/for that reason'. سپهبد is the title 'spahbed' (the military commander). وزین روی is an adjectival phrase 'with a dignified/grave face' (وزین = 'dignified/grave', روی = 'face'), and شاه names the addressee 'the king' — the clause is nominal and the verbal action (approaching/speaking) is implied, as often in epic narrative.
segments.s676-g121: This is two coordinated pieces: an archaic past verb form plus a following clause. بگستهم is a poetic/preterite form (prefixed بـ) meaning “spread/opened/arrayed” (verb first as is common in epic style). و is the coordinating conjunction “and.” بندوی (the bondman/servant) is the actor of the next verb; فرمود is a past tense reporting/command verb “said/ordered.” شاه follows as the addressee/related title — word order here (verb then agent/title) reflects classical Persian flexibility where the verb may precede or be separated from its agent.
segments.s676-g122: که introduces reported speech or a subordinate clause (“that …”). تا here functions as a purposive/subjunctive marker meaning roughly “that/so that” leading into the action. بر + نهادند is a compound where بر is the preposition ‘on/upon’ and نهادند is the past plural of نهادن “to place/put”; together بر نهادند = “they put on.” ز + آهن is a genitive/material expression “of iron,” and کلاه is the object “helmet/hat.” The whole phrase gives the commanded action: “that they put on helmets of iron.”
segments.s676-g123: چنین is an adverb “thus/so” modifying the following action. با introduces accompaniment “with.” بزرگان is a plural noun “the great ones/elders,” followed by the epithet روشن‌روان (a poetic compound adjective “bright‑souled,” an honorific). Word order places the descriptive epithet after the noun, a common Persian pattern.
segments.s676-g124: همی‌راند is an imperfective/habitual past form (prefix همی‑ indicating ongoing or habitual past action) meaning “was driving/was leading/pressed on.” تا here means “as far as/up to” indicating extent or destination. چشمه نهروان is the place phrase “the spring (چشمه) of Nahrawan,” the goal of the movement.
segments.s676-g125: طلایه is the subject “vanguard/forefront.” ببهرام is a prepositional/relational form meaning “to/for Bahram” (the prefix بـ giving role/assignment). شد ناگزیر is a compound past: شد “became” plus ناگزیر “compelled/inevitable,” so together “became unavoidable/was forced.” The clause states that command/charge of the vanguard devolved to Bahram.
segments.s676-g126: که begins a subordinate/explanatory clause “for/that.” آمد is simple past “came.” سپه is the actor “the host/army.” The phrase بر دو پرتاب تیر (here given without the numeral in the word list) describes manner: بر + پرتاب تیر = “with a throw/launch of arrows”; the sense is that the army approached while sending two castings/volleys of arrows — پرتاب تیر = “launch/throw of arrows.”
segments.s676-g127: چوبشنید is the fused temporal expression (چو + شنید) “when he heard/on hearing.” The proper name بهرام follows as the actor. لشکر is the object or complement “the army,” and براند is the past form of راندن meaning here “drove/drave on/put in motion” (classical narrative often places verb after subject/object, so be attentive to flexibility). The clause means ‘On hearing, Bahram set the army in motion/advanced the host.’
segments.s676-g128: جهاندیدگان is the definite plural noun “the experienced/veteran soldiers” (a conventional epithet). را is the definite object marker marking them as the direct object. برخویش is a compound prepositional phrase: بر + خویش = “to himself/toward himself.” خواند is the simple past of خواندن used in the sense “to call/summon.” Together: “he summoned the veterans to himself.”
segments.s676-g97: وگر is the conditional 'if' (archaic poetic form). جنگ جوید is a present/optative sense 'seeks/desires war' with جوید as an older 3rd‑person present/present-subjunctive. منم is the emphatic copula 'I am' (من + enclitic ـم), and جنگ جوی is a compound noun 'war-seeker' = 'warrior' functioning as the predicate of منم.
segments.s676-g98: سپه را is the direct object 'the army' marked by را for emphasis; the following phrase uses archaic/poetic word order: بروی اندر آریم روی functions as a result clause meaning 'we bring/set (the army) ... face-to-face'. آریم is a present/subjunctive plural 'we bring/set', اندر is the preposition 'in/among', and روی ('face') is the locative/complement giving the sense 'face-to-face' — literally 'we bring the host onto (its) face.'
segments.s676-g99: همه کاردانان = 'all the experts/wise men' (subject). بدین داستان is a fixed phrase 'on this tale / regarding this story' (بدین = به + این 'with/at this'), placed before the verb for topical emphasis.
segments.s676-g100: کجا گفت is an embedded question 'where was it said/told?' (کجا = where; گفت here represents 'said/told' in reported speech). گشتند is the past plural 'they became' and همداستان is a predicate adjective 'of one mind / agreed', so the clause means 'they became of one mind' while also asking 'where was it said?'.
segments.s676-g101: بزرگان = 'the great ones / nobles' (subject). برو آفرین is an exclamatory phrase ('Bravo!' or an acclamation) where آفرین is the interjection 'bravo / well done' and خواندند (past plural) here means 'they cried out / uttered'.
segments.s676-g102: ورا = 'to him' (indirect object/pronoun). شهریار زمین is a title-like noun phrase 'king / lord of the land' (شهریار 'king' + زمین 'land, country'). خواندند repeats the past plural 'they called/named' — together: 'they called him king of the land.'
segments.s676-g103: همی‌گفت is an iterative imperfect 'kept saying / would say' (habitual or repeated speech). هرکس = 'each person / whoever', and که‌ای شهریار is an address: که here introduces the quoted utterance and ای (vocative) + شهریار is 'O king / O sovereign' — the phrase introduces the blessing spoken to the ruler.
segments.s676-g104: زتو = 'from you' (prepositional phrase placed before the predicate). دور بادا is an optative construction: دور = 'far', بادا = 'may it be' (a wish/optative particle). بد روزگار = 'evil/bad fortune' (بد modifying روزگار 'fate/fortune'). Together: 'May bad fortune be far from you.'
segments.s676-g49: This is a nominal clause with the predicate introduced by the copula است (is). سیم‌کش (literally “silver‑drawer/‑worker”) is the subject/name and کلیله here functions as a proper name/title in predicate position. ودمنه وزیر is a coordinated noun phrase: و‑دمنه (and Dâmneh) as the name followed by وزیر (vizier) as an appositive/title. Word order is Subject — PredicateNominal — apposition.
segments.s676-g50: چون begins a causal clause meaning “since/because.” او is the 3rd‑person pronoun “he.” رای زن here denotes ‘counsel‑giver’ (an adviser) and کس ندارد دبیر states lack (ندارد = does not have) of any person (کس) as دبیر (secretary/scribe). The negative possession structure کس ندارد literally 'he has no one' links back to او (he) and completes the causal clause introduced by چون.
segments.s676-g51: ازان پس is a temporal connector meaning “after that/thereupon.” ببندوی is an older/poetic 2nd‑person imperative/optative form meaning “(you) bind/fasten (him)!” and گستهم is the proper name Gostaham (the addressee). گفت is the past tense verb “said.” The order places the command form before the addressee name and finally the reporting verb گفت (literally: 'After that, “Bind!” he said to Gostaham').
segments.s676-g52: که introduces a subordinate content clause 'that/for' explaining the reason. ما = 'we'; با غم و رنج is a prepositional phrase 'with sorrow and suffering'; گشتیم is the perfect/past of گشتن used here as 'we have become' or 'we have come to be'; جفت (literally 'pair') figuratively means 'yoked/paired with' and completes the image — 'we are paired with sorrow and hardship.'
segments.s676-g53: This segment lists names/epithets joined by و (and). چوگردوی and شاپور are proper names; the second چون here functions as a conjunctive particle meaning 'and likewise/like' before اندیان (another name/group). The sequence is a list of persons (or groups) introduced without a verb — a typical poetic enumeration.
segments.s676-g54: سپهدار is a title meaning 'commander' and ارمینیه specifies the region 'Armenia/Armenian' in adjectival position; رادمان is a proper name/epithet attached in apposition. The phrase thus names Radman as the sepahdar of Armenia — Title/region followed by the name in apposition.
segments.s676-g55: نشستند is a past plural verb 'they sat' (here indicating a group took their seats). با شاه ایران places the action 'with the king of Iran' (با = with, شاه ایران = king of Iran), and براز is a name in apposition to شاه ایران (Baraz). Word order is Verb — prepositional phrase complement — appositive name.
segments.s676-g56: A three‑word epithet phrase modifying the subjects: بزرگان (the great ones) is the head noun; فرزانه (wise) and رزمساز (lit. 'war‑maker'/'one who makes war', i.e. warrior/leaders in war) are attributive adjectives following the noun as is common in Persian poetic style, forming the compound epithet 'the great, wise makers/masters of war.'
segments.s676-g57: چنین (an adverb) is fronted and means 'thus/so'; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (3rd sing.) following the adverb, and خسرو is the subject placed after the verb (classical Persian permits V–S order). بدان مهتران is a prepositional phrase 'to those chiefs' where ب is the preposition (به 'to') and دان here functions as the demonstrative/old-form 'آن' ('that/those') attached before مهتران (the addressee). The whole segment sets up direct speech.
segments.s676-g58: که‌ای is a poetic/exclamatory particle: که + ای used to call attention (vocative). سرفرازان و جنگ آوران are two coordinated vocatives ('the proud/victorious ones' and 'the war‑bringers/warriors'), joined by و 'and'. In context this is the direct address that follows the verb of speaking.
segments.s676-g59: هرآن introduces an indefinite/relative idea 'whosoever' (classical use), مغز 'brain' = 'mind' is the topical noun, and کو functions here as an archaic relative element ('who/which') linking the noun to the following modifier. را is the object/contrast marker attached to مغز, and خرد روشنست is the predicate 'wisdom is bright/clear' (روشنست = روشن است, adjective + copula fused). Word order is classical: relative phrase → predicate.
segments.s676-g60: زدانش is a fused prepositional phrase = ز + دانش (from/by that knowledge) where ز = از 'from/by' and دانش = 'knowledge'. یکی 'one/a single' quantifies the following noun; بر is the preposition 'on/upon', تن‎ش is 'his body' with the enclitic possessive ش = 'his', and جوشنست = جوشن است 'is a cuirass' (adjective/noun + copula contracted). Together: 'by that knowledge one cuirass is on his body' (metaphor: knowledge as armor).
segments.s676-g61: کس at the clause start is an indefinite negative 'no one' (here: nobody can do X). آنرا is the direct object 'that (it)' with the object marker -را. نبرد is the verbal root form used here in the sense 'takes / carries off' (classical usage; the literal dictionary entry may vary), and مگر introduces the exception 'except/unless'. تیغ مرگ is the nominative phrase 'the sword of death' — the only thing that can take it. The clause thus means 'no one takes it except the sword of death.'
segments.s676-g62: شود is the present/future copular/auxiliary 'becomes' (classical simple verb). موم 'wax' is the predicative noun; ازان = از + آن 'from that' (i.e. from that wound); زخم 'wound' and پولاد 'steel' appear together in a metaphorical clause, and ترگ ('rag/shred') indicates the steel is rendered as if torn to shreds. The poetic image: by that wound the once‑hard steel acts like wax and is rent to pieces. Word order places the verb first for emphasis (classical style).
segments.s676-g63: کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame. من is the explicit subject 'I'. بسال is a comparative measure 'by a year' (i.e. differing by a year), and ازشما is the comparative marker 'from/than you' (classical comparative construction). کهترم is an older comparative adjective meaning 'less/other in age/rank' — together the clause expresses 'I differ from you by a year' (classical word order places the comparative phrase before the predicate).
segments.s676-g64: برای here means 'for/on account of' or 'as for' and introduces the object phrase جوانی جهان 'the youth of the world' (جوانی = youth; جهان = the world/realm). نسپرم is the negative first‑person present/subjunctive form of سپردن (نَسَپَرم) 'I will not entrust/hand over' — ن + سپرم, with final -م marking 1st person. The clause thus states 'I will not entrust the world to youth.'
